titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Sidney A. Olson Papers
titlestmt.author: by Janet Lopes

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Tuesday December 23, 2025 at 23:01

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is restricted until January 1, 2026.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged into two series: I. Writings, notes, correspondence, and clippings, 1923-2024; and II. Photographic prints: 1934-1948.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Sidney A. Olson (1908-1995) was an American journalist and writer, born and raised in Utah. During World War II, he served as a war correspondent for TIME, covering the German front from December 1944 to May 1945. He was a Senior Editor for TIME, LIFE, and Fortune. He was a staff writer, White House correspondent, and City Editor at The Washington Post and spent the end of his career in advertising.

In April of 1945, Olson was one of the first people to enter the Dachau concentration camp as it was being liberated. His World War II dispatches written for TIME and LIFE were touted for their detail, insights about military strategy, and observations about human suffering.
Olson--along with other correspondents--was honored for his work by U. S. Forces and the U. S. Secretary of War.
After the war, Olson co-anchored the first live televised broadcast of the 1948 Presidential race. In the 1950s, Olson turned to Hollywood, public relations, and advertising.

Olson's legacy reflects a rich history of work for the Washington Post, with a byline on more than 700 articles, mostly about politics. His second career in advertising spanned more than 20 years before his retirement in 1973.
Diagnosed with Parkinson's disease in the 1980s, Olson died in 1995 in Darien, Connecticut.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Sidney A. Olson papers. General Collection, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: The Sidney A. Olson papers contain writings, notes, logs, correspondence, photographic prints, newspaper clippings, and other papers by, or related to, the work of journalist Sidney A. Olson. The papers reflect Olson's life and career beginning in the 1920s as a young adult entering the University of Utah. There are examples of Olson's early writings under the pen name Incamusket, as well as his interests in books and the creative process. Papers during this period include diaries along with detailed notes on his social life.

From the 1930s-1940s, there are many examples of Olson's work at The Washington Post and, ultimately, TIME . A large amount of correspondence details his observations as a White House journalist and, later on, as a WWII correspondent, revealed in letters and cables to TIME co-founder, Henry Luce and news bureau chief David Hulburd.

Highlights of his pre-war reporting include a memo from John McCloy who would go on to be U. S. administrator of postwar Germany. Of note is Olson's reporting on the liberation of Nuremberg and the Dachau Concentration Camp.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Sidney A. Olson papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains writings from Olson's college years in the 1920s and well as into his journalism career and beyond throughout the 1950s. Other papers include diaries, clippings, correspondence detailing Olson's coverage of WWII and politics.

did.unittitle: High school diploma, college transcript, and ROTC certificate
Directory and art by girlfriend, Fran Rager
Diary entry, correspondence and other papers
Marriage license, FDR inauguration, and other papers
Correspondence from Olson to wife, Zembra Holmgren
Nine U. S. insignia and one patch
Correspondence to wife, Zembra Holmgren, and children
Letter of recommendation and press conference pass
Orders to head out to Canadian front
Cable via Navy Radio to TIME
"A Thing of Beauty", copy and cable to David Hulburd
Cable to David Hulburd, attention Henry Luce
V-mail to Sidney A. Olson from his mother
"A Letter from the Publisher", copy and cable
Correspondence from James Quirk to wife
"Searching for the Heart", copy and cable
"The Collapse of the Nazi Empire is a Fantastic Show" annotated typescript
Various articles and emails by others
Correspondence to Olson from Henry Luce, copy
Correspondence from George Warden, GS Chief
Correspondence from Col. G. S. C. James C. Welch
Correspondence from Ralph "Del" Paine of TIME
War Department award certificate and article
Notes for WWII novel and outlines
TIME newsletter, Fortune article and Christmas cards
Paramount article, first draft and printed article
Travel permits and other papers relating to the Berlin airlift
Billings' diary account of firing Olson
Correspondence to Henry Luce and severance stub
Correspondence from Henry Luce regarding Olson's firing
Page from Bob Elson's blook regarding Olson, copy
Correspondence, clippings, and notes related to Paramount

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Maintains order found at the time of acquisition.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes photographs related to political conventions, newsroom activities, Olson's time at The Washington Post and TIME and as a war correspondent.

did.unittitle: Election night, Sidney A. Olson interviewing Jim Farley
Sidney Olson with mother Sigrid, daughter Whitney, and son John
Clare Booth Luce with Sidney Olson in Heidelberg
Sidney Olson, war correspondent, profile picture
Sidney Olson, assistant managing editor at TIME
Claude Mahoney of Wall Street Journal with Sidney Olson
William Lemke meeting with reporters on farm bill
Reporters at Chesapeake Bay meeting with FDR
Sidney A. Olson traveling from Utah to Washington
Wallace Kirkland photographs for LIFE , likely at GOP Convention
